section.title: A Good Name
section.sectionRationale: These sixteen verses close the first Solomonic collection, 10:1 to 22:16, before the book turns to the Sayings of the Wise. The chapter opens by ranking a good name above riches, moves through the shared humanity of rich and poor under their Maker, the training of a child, and closes with warnings against oppressing the poor for gain.
section.thesis: A good name and the fear of the LORD outweigh riches, since rich and poor alike are made by the same LORD, and wisdom trains children early, gives generously, and refuses to build a future on oppression of the poor.
section.frameNote: Four clusters: a good name above riches, and the common Maker of rich and poor; prudence, humility, and the discipline of a child; generosity, hospitality, and driving out the mocker; the deep pit of the adulteress, and warnings against oppressing the poor.

units.label: A good name above riches
units.note: the common Maker of rich and poor

verses.text: A good name is more desirable than great riches; favor is better than silver and gold.
The rich and the poor have this in common: The LORD is Maker of them all.
The prudent see danger and take cover, but the simple keep going and suffer the consequences.
The rewards of humility and the {{yirah|fear}} of the LORD are wealth and honor and life.

units.label: Prudence and the training of a child
units.note: guard your soul, train up a child

verses.text: Thorns and snares lie on the path of the perverse; he who guards his soul stays far from them.
Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it.
The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender.
He who sows injustice will reap disaster, and the rod of his fury will be destroyed.

units.label: Generosity and driving out the mocker
units.note: a generous man will be blessed

verses.text: A generous man will be blessed, for he shares his bread with the poor.
Drive out the mocker, and conflict will depart; even quarreling and insults will cease.
He who loves a pure heart and gracious lips will have the king for a friend.
The LORD's eyes keep watch over knowledge, but He frustrates the words of the faithless.

units.label: Warnings against oppressing the poor
units.note: the deep pit and the rod of discipline

verses.text: The slacker says, "There is a lion outside! I will be slain in the streets!"
The mouth of an adulteress is a deep pit; he who is under the wrath of the LORD will fall into it.
Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of discipline drives it far from him.
Oppressing the poor to enrich oneself or giving gifts to the rich will surely lead to poverty.

yirah.gloss: Fear, reverent awe of God. Verse 4 pairs this fear with humility as the twin roots that yield wealth, honor, and life, the same fear named in Proverbs 1:7 as the beginning of knowledge, showing that true reward flows from reverence, not from grasping or self-promotion.

basic.context: This short section closes the long first collection of Solomon's proverbs. It opens with a striking priority, a good name and God's favor outrank silver and gold, and grounds human equality in the fact that the LORD made both rich and poor. From there it moves through the training of children and closes with sharp warnings against oppressing the poor for personal gain.
basic.hermeneutics: These are wisdom generalizations describing how life under God tends to reward humility, generosity, and careful child training, and tends to punish injustice and oppression. Verse 6, 'train up a child... he will not depart,' is widely misread as an ironclad promise; Proverbs' own genre and the broader witness of Scripture, including children who reject good training, show it describes a normal pattern of formation rather than an unconditional guarantee.
basic.originalLanguages: Yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 4, is paired with humility as the root of true reward, wealth, honor, and life, echoing the refrain that runs through the whole book. The chapter's insistence that the LORD is Maker of both rich and poor, verse 2, uses the same verb for God's creative work found in Genesis, grounding human dignity in shared origin rather than social status.
basic.typology: The good name prized above riches, verse 1, anticipates the name of Jesus, above every name, in which alone true and lasting favor is found, Philippians 2:9-11. The common Maker of rich and poor, verse 2, foreshadows the gospel's leveling of all people before the cross, where neither wealth nor poverty grants standing before God, only grace received by faith.
basic.christ: Verse 1's good name, worth more than silver and gold, finds its ultimate referent in the name of Jesus, given the name above every name, at which every knee will bow, Philippians 2:9 to 11, the name in which alone true and lasting favor is secured. And verse 2's declaration that the LORD is Maker of both rich and poor anticipates the gospel truth that at the foot of the cross all human distinctions of wealth and status collapse before the one Savior who died for both, Galatians 3:28.
basic.god: God is the Maker of rich and poor alike, verse 2, refusing any human hierarchy of worth based on wealth, and He rewards humility and reverent fear with true wealth, honor, and life, verse 4. He watches over knowledge and frustrates the schemes of the faithless, verse 12, and His anger burns against those who oppress the poor to enrich themselves, verse 16.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians 2:9-11 exalts the name above every name, the ultimate good name of verse 1. Galatians 3:28 echoes the shared humanity of rich and poor before God, verse 2. Ephesians 6:4 develops verse 6's training of children with a father's tender restraint. James 2:1-9 echoes verse 16's warning against favoring the rich and oppressing the poor.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 22:1 to 16 prizes a good name, humble fear of the LORD, and generous care for the poor over wealth gained by injustice. The whole story of Scripture answers with the name of Jesus, exalted above every name, in whom rich and poor alike, sharing one Maker, find the true and lasting favor no money can buy.

context.literary: These sixteen verses form the closing unit of the first Solomonic collection, 10:1 to 22:16, before Proverbs 22:17 introduces a new section, the Sayings of the Wise, with a distinct introduction and style.
context.historical: In a society where a name carried a family's honor across generations and where debt slavery, verse 7, was a real and pressing danger, these proverbs address urgent, practical realities: reputation, generosity, child rearing, and the abuse of economic power over the vulnerable.
context.canonical: Verse 2's insistence that the LORD made both rich and poor threads through Job 31:15 and reappears in James 2, part of Scripture's consistent refusal to let wealth determine a person's worth before God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To rank character, reputation, and reverence for God above material wealth, to instruct parents in forming children early, and to warn sharply against enriching oneself by oppressing the poor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 6 and 9 prescribe conduct directly, train a child, be generous, while verse 7, 'the borrower is slave to the lender,' mainly describes an economic reality rather than commanding debt as ideal; readers should distinguish direct commands from honest observations of how power and money tend to work.

debates.question: Is verse 6, 'train up a child... when he is old he will not depart,' a guaranteed outcome for faithful parenting?

views.label: Yes, it promises that faithful training secures a child's future faithfulness
views.case: The verse's unconditional phrasing, 'he will not depart,' is read by some as a firm promise attached to diligent parental training.

views.label: It is a general wisdom principle, not an ironclad promise
views.case: Like other Proverbs sayings, it describes the normal outcome of early, consistent moral formation without guaranteeing every individual case; Scripture itself records godly parents whose children strayed, such as Samuel's sons, 1 Samuel 8:3, showing the proverb states a pattern, not a mechanical law.

debates.bearing: Reading the verse as a general principle rather than an unconditional guarantee both honors the real value of faithful parenting and protects grieving parents of wayward adult children from false guilt, since Scripture elsewhere shows children retain real moral responsibility of their own.

study.originalLanguages: Yirah, fear of the LORD, verse 4, is paired with humility as the twin source of true wealth, honor, and life, tying this closing section back to the book's opening motto in 1:7. The Hebrew verb for 'Maker,' verse 2, echoes the creation language of Genesis, grounding the dignity of both rich and poor in the same divine act of making.

typology.fulfillment: The name of Jesus, above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.

typology.type: The LORD as Maker of rich and poor alike, 22:2
typology.fulfillment: The gospel's leveling of all people before the cross, Galatians 3:28.

study.christ: The good name of verse 1, worth more than silver and gold, points forward to the name of Jesus, exalted above every name, before which every knee will one day bow, Philippians 2:9 to 11, the only name that secures lasting favor with God. Verse 2's declaration that the LORD made both rich and poor anticipates the gospel's own leveling work, where neither wealth nor poverty grants any advantage before the cross, only grace received through faith unites rich and poor as one new people in Christ, Galatians 3:28.
study.god: God is the common Maker of rich and poor, verse 2, refusing to let wealth define human worth, and He rewards humility and reverent fear with true wealth, honor, and life, verse 4. He keeps watch over knowledge and frustrates the schemes of the faithless, verse 12, and stands firmly against those who oppress the poor to enrich themselves, verse 16.

crossReferences.note: The name above every name, the ultimate answer to the good name prized in Proverbs 22:1.
Rich and poor united as one in Christ, echoing the shared Maker of Proverbs 22:2.
Fathers are to bring up children in the training of the Lord, developing Proverbs 22:6.
Warns against favoritism toward the rich and dishonoring the poor, echoing Proverbs 22:16.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 22:1 to 16 prizes a good name, humility, reverence for God, and generosity over wealth gained by oppressing the poor. This whole vision is fulfilled in Jesus, whose name above every name secures the favor no riches could buy, and under whom rich and poor, sharing one Maker, become one people through the gospel.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 say is more desirable than great riches?

options.text: A good name, and favor better than silver and gold.
options.note: Reputation and God's favor are ranked above material wealth.

options.text: A large inheritance.
options.note: The verse ranks a good name above riches, not equates them.

options.text: Physical strength.
options.note: Strength is not the topic of this verse.

options.text: A good name, valued about equally with riches.
options.note: The verse ranks a good name clearly above riches, not merely equal to them.

quiz.q: What do rich and poor have in common according to verse 2?

options.text: The LORD is Maker of them all.
options.note: Shared origin in God grounds equal human dignity regardless of wealth.

options.text: They share equal amounts of wealth over time.
options.note: The verse addresses shared origin, not economic equality.

options.text: They are equally prone to laziness.
options.note: This is not the point the verse is making.

options.text: They will eventually become equally wealthy.
options.note: The verse's point is about common creation by God, not eventual economic leveling.

quiz.q: What does verse 6 counsel about training a child?

options.text: Train up a child in the way he should go, so that when he is old he will not depart from it.
options.note: Early, consistent moral formation is commended as normally shaping a person's whole life.

options.text: Children should be left to choose their own way without guidance.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the verse commends.

options.text: Only formal education matters for a child's future.
options.note: The verse speaks broadly of moral and spiritual training, not formal education specifically.

options.text: Training guarantees a child's future choices with no exceptions.
options.note: Read alongside the rest of Scripture, this states a general pattern rather than an unconditional, exception-free guarantee.

quiz.q: What does verse 9 say about the generous man?

options.text: He will be blessed, for he shares his bread with the poor.
options.note: Generosity toward the poor is directly tied to blessing.

options.text: He will lose everything by giving too much away.
options.note: This is the opposite of the promise in the verse.

options.text: He should give only to those who can repay him.
options.note: The verse commends sharing bread with the poor, who by definition cannot repay.

options.text: He is blessed only if his generosity is publicly recognized.
options.note: The verse attaches no condition of public recognition to the blessing described.

quiz.q: What warning does verse 16 give about wealth?

options.text: Oppressing the poor to enrich oneself, or giving gifts to the rich, will surely lead to poverty.
options.note: Unjust methods of gaining wealth ultimately backfire.

options.text: All forms of wealth accumulation lead to poverty.
options.note: The verse specifically targets unjust methods, not wealth in general.

options.text: Giving gifts to the rich always increases one's own wealth.
options.note: The verse says the opposite, that this leads to poverty.

options.text: Oppressing the poor is only wrong if it becomes publicly known.
options.note: The verse states the consequence as a moral certainty, not contingent on public discovery.

questions: Verse 1 ranks a good name above riches. Where are you currently tempted to trade your integrity for financial gain or advancement?
Verse 2 says the LORD is Maker of both rich and poor. How does this truth challenge the way you view or treat people of different economic status?
Verse 6 speaks of training a child in the way he should go. If you are raising or influencing children, what is one habit of training you need to be more consistent about?
Verse 9 blesses the one who shares bread with the poor. What would practical, costly generosity look like for you this month?

Verse 16 warns against enriching yourself by oppressing the poor. Are there any of your financial habits, spending, business, or investment choices, that indirectly depend on someone else's exploitation?

translationNotes.note: 'A good name' translates shem tov, literally 'a good name,' a phrase carrying the weight of reputation, family honor, and covenant identity, not mere popularity.
'In the way he should go' can also be rendered 'according to his way,' possibly meaning suited to the child's own bent or stage, a nuance debated among Hebrew scholars.
'The mouth of an adulteress is a deep pit' anticipates the extended warning against the adulteress developed further in the discourses of chapters 2, 5, and 7.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 22:1-16. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
